(died 10[[1]](#cite_note-1) or 17 February 661), also known as **Saint Finan**, was an Irish [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk"), trained at [Iona Abbey](/wiki/Iona_Abbey "Iona Abbey") in Scotland, who became the second [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") from 651 until 661.

**Finan of Lindisfarne** (died 10[[1]](#cite_note-1) or 17 February 661), also known as **Saint Finan**, was an Irish [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk"), trained at [Iona Abbey](/wiki/Iona_Abbey "Iona Abbey") in Scotland, who became the second [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") from 651 until 661.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finan_of_Lindisfarne&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Finan was appointed to Lindisfarne in 651,[[2]](#cite_note-Handbook219-2) to succeed [Aidan](/wiki/Aidan_of_Lindisfarne "Aidan of Lindisfarne").

Originally from Ireland, he built on Lindisfarne, a cathedral "in the Irish fashion", employing hewn oak, with a thatched roof. Later Archbishop [Theodore of Tarsus](/wiki/Theodore_of_Tarsus "Theodore of Tarsus") dedicated to St. Peter.[[3]](#cite_note-northern-3) Finan also founded [St. Mary's Priory](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Lighthouse "St Mary's Lighthouse") at the mouth of the River Tyne.[[4]](#cite_note-grattan-4) He also participated in the founding of [Gilling Abbey](/wiki/Gilling_Abbey "Gilling Abbey"), established by Queen [Eanflæd](/wiki/Eanfl%C3%A6d "Eanflæd") in memory of her kinsman [Oswine of Deira](/wiki/Oswine_of_Deira "Oswine of Deira").[[3]](#cite_note-northern-3)

He converted the [kings](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") [Sigebert of Essex](/wiki/Sigeberht_II_of_Essex "Sigeberht II of Essex")[[5]](#cite_note-Walsh203-5) and [Peada of the Middle Angles](/wiki/Peada_of_Mercia "Peada of Mercia") to [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity").[[6]](#cite_note-Kirby79-6) [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") is the main source for Finan's life.[[5]](#cite_note-Walsh203-5) He is specially noted by Bede as having borne an important part in the conversion of the northern Saxons.[[7]](#cite_note-webb-7)

The breviary of Aberdeen styles him "a man of venerable life, a bishop of great sanctity, an eloquent teacher ... remarkable for his training in virtue and his liberal education, surpassing all his equals in every manner of knowledge as well as in circumspection and prudence, but chiefly devoting himself to good works and presenting in his life, a most apt example of virtue".[[4]](#cite_note-grattan-4) Finan ordained St. [Cedd](/wiki/Cedd "Cedd") bishop of the East-Saxons, having called two other bishops to assist at his consecration.

The Abbey of Whitby, his chief foundation, was the scene of the [Synod of Whitby](/wiki/Synod_of_Whitby "Synod of Whitby"), which resulted in the withdrawal of the Irish monks from Lindisfarne.[[4]](#cite_note-grattan-4)

Finan was active for some time at a monastery on Church Island on [Lough Currane](/wiki/Lough_Currane "Lough Currane") in [County Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry"); today it is known as St. Finan's Church. To the south of the lake is Inis Uasal (Noble Island), an island which is dedicated to him.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Finan died in 661,[[2]](#cite_note-Handbook219-2) and was buried at Lindisfarne, having held that see ten years.

## Veneration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Finan_of_Lindisfarne&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Veneration")]

Finan's feast day is celebrated upon 9 January.[[7]](#cite_note-webb-7) He is venerated in the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church") (celebrated on 17 February), and the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church").
